output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.85, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Summary: Critique of the introduction with concrete revision recommendations; the section overclaims novelty, leans on under-specified evidence, and ends on promotional rather than scientific notes.

Major issues

1. The "first" claims are almost certainly wrong and should be removed or heavily qualified.
   - "the first robotic control framework to route action queries to differently-sized VLMs" and "the first cost-aware VLM cascading mechanism for embodied agents" are strong priority claims. Model cascading and routing are well-established in the LLM-serving literature (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, Tabi, cascaded inference, etc.), and several recent embodied-AI papers have explored hierarchical or small-then-large VLM/LLM querying (e.g., SayCan-style hierarchies, DoReMi, Inner Monologue with verifier escalation, hybrid local/cloud robot policies). Unless you have done a comprehensive prior-work sweep and can name the closest neighbors here, drop "first" and instead state the specific delta (e.g., "a learned per-step difficulty estimator that routes between an on-device 3B VLM and a 70B cloud VLM without rollout lookahead").
   - The third contribution ("meta-modeling … is a viable design pattern") restates a known idea (cascades, speculative decoding, verifier models). Either reframe as an empirical demonstration in the embodied setting or cut.

2. The headline evidence is thin for the strength of the conclusions.
   - 91.4% vs. 92.1% is reported as a single point estimate with no confidence interval, no seed variance, and no number of trials per task. With 40 tasks, a 0.7-point gap could easily be noise. Report n_rollouts per task, seeds, and a CI or paired test.
   - The 23% routing rate, 4.2× cost reduction, and 2.7× latency reduction are interdependent (cost scales with routing rate and model price ratio). State the assumed pricing/latency model so readers can reproduce the numbers, and clarify whether latency includes network RTT for the 70B call.
   - The always-small baseline (71.8%) is the only comparator besides always-large. Add at least: (a) random routing at 23%, (b) an oracle upper bound, (c) an entropy/confidence-thresholded routing baseline from the small VLM itself. Without these, the meta-classifier's value is unestablished.
   - Training labels are described as "comparing small- and large-model rollouts against ground-truth success." Specify: per-step labels or per-trajectory? How is credit assigned across steps? This is the central design choice and is currently opaque.

3. Generalization claims outrun the evidence.
   - All results are on one arm (Franka Panda), one domain (tabletop kitchen manipulation), one teleop dataset, and one model pair (3B vs. 70B). The closing paragraph asserts the method "generalizes beyond manipulation" and "we expect RoboRouter to become a standard component" — neither is supported. Either delete those sentences or restate as future work / open questions (e.g., "whether the meta-classifier transfers across embodiments and model pairs remains open").
   - The training distribution (kitchen teleop) and evaluation distribution ("40 long-horizon tabletop tasks") should be explicitly stated as same/different. If same, the success number is in-distribution and should be flagged.

4. Logical gaps to close.
   - You motivate the work with "frontier-scale VLMs … prohibitively expensive," then evaluate against a 70B model. 70B is mid-scale by frontier standards; either soften the motivation or justify why 70B stands in for frontier cost/latency.
   - You claim routing "from observation features alone, without expensive rollout lookahead" is a contribution, but you also say labels come from rollouts. The training-time vs. inference-time asymmetry is worth one explicit sentence — currently a reader could miss it.
   - The meta-classifier input is "current observation and the symbolic task description," but the abstract earlier says "per-step task difficulty." Clarify whether the classifier sees history/progress or only the instantaneous state; this matters for long-horizon claims.

Minor / prose

- "differently-sized VLMs" → "VLMs of different sizes" reads more naturally.
- "on-board 3B-parameter VLM" vs. "70B-parameter cloud-hosted VLM" — the on-board claim implies hardware constraints; state the target platform once (e.g., Jetson Orin) or drop "on-board."
- The contribution list and the closing paragraph overlap with the abstract paragraph; tighten by either folding contributions into the opening or removing the standalone list.
- "drop the layer in front of any existing VLM-based policy with minimal integration effort" is marketing language; replace with the actual API surface (input/output signature of the router).
- Citations: the section currently has none. An introduction making a "first" claim must cite the cascading/routing literature it is differentiating from, plus the closest embodied-AI baselines.

Suggested restructure

1. Problem (cost/latency of frontier VLM queries in long-horizon robotics).
2. Idea (learned per-step router between small on-device and large cloud VLM).
3. What's new vs. existing cascades and existing hierarchical robot policies — name them.
4. Setup in one sentence (Franka, 40 tasks, 3B/70B pair, 12k-trajectory training set).
5. Headline result with the most informative comparison (vs. confidence-thresholded baseline and oracle, not just always-large/always-small).
6. Scope and limits (single embodiment, single model pair, in-domain eval) — stated up front, not buried.

Uncertainty disclosure: I have not verified the prior-art landscape against a current literature search; the specific neighbor papers I named (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, Tabi, SayCan, DoReMi, Inner Monologue) are plausible reference points but you should confirm exact relevance and check for 2024–2026 embodied-routing work I may be unaware of. The statistical critique assumes the omissions in the text reflect the actual paper; if CIs and additional baselines exist later in the manuscript, fold them into the intro rather than adding them.
